headlineSummary: OpenAI drew the most clearly positive reactions, Anthropic was mixed on high-volume evidence, and Gemini had a smaller positive note with limited evidence [E4][E1][E3].

ranking.summary: Anthropic landed in mixed territory on high-volume evidence: some comments praised Claude for useful coding and fast task completion, while others criticized failures on tolerances, trust, and pricing [E1][E2][E5][E6][E8][E9].

Google Gemini had a positive mention with limited evidence, with one commenter praising its image understanding and smooth outputs while noting that its creativity can be less ideal for precise CAD work [E3].
OpenAI was viewed positively overall, with comments praising Codex and GPT for strong practical usefulness and speed, though one note criticized pricing [E4][E7][E8][E6].

evidence.excerpt: I gave Claude pictures of the pill-shaped hole by itself and with my digital calipers in the long and short directions. Gave it a short prompt and it gave me an openscad model with everything parametrized. I printed with no changes in tpu and it was nearly perfect on the first try.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly credits Claude with producing a parametrized OpenSCAD model that was nearly perfect on the first print.

evidence.excerpt: I tried Claude code designing a snap fit, vase mode printed box. Ultimately didn't work out, it couldn't get the tolerances right and kept designing features that wouldn't print in vase mode.

annotations.rationale: This is a direct complaint about Claude Code failing at tolerances and printable design constraints.

evidence.excerpt: - Gemini models are the least jagged in my experience and have the best image understanding - Gemini models are also the most creative (which may be undesirable if you want precise CAD part)

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly praises Gemini models for image understanding, smoothness, and creativity, while noting a minor caveat.

evidence.excerpt: You can get a taste of this today yourself with Codex Security. I turned it on just as an experiment and in less than a week it has now become essential to all of us. I was shocked how accurate it is, how many security issues it found in existing code, how it continually finds them as we commit

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says Codex Security became essential within a week and praises its accuracy and usefulness in finding issues.

evidence.excerpt: I’m not sure how to reconcile anthropic’s update / some of the exuberant comments here with recent feedback like the following from curl maintainer Daniel Steinberg: “I see no evidence that this setup [Mythos] finds issues to any particular higher or more advanced degree than the other tools have done before Mythos.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly questions Anthropic's update by citing external feedback that Mythos shows no meaningful advantage over prior tools.

evidence.excerpt: That makes DeepSeek v4 Pro extremely cheap compared to others, even in the same category. Look at these prices per million outputs tokens: DeepSeek V4 Pro: $0.87 Qwen 3.7 Max: $7.50 Grok 4.3: $2.50 GLM 1.5: $3.08 Opus 4.7: $25.00 GPT-5.5: $30.00

annotations.rationale: By explicitly listing Opus 4.7 at a much higher price in a comment about competitors being extremely cheap, the commenter frames Anthropic's pricing negatively.
By explicitly listing GPT-5.5 at a much higher price in a comment about competitors being extremely cheap, the commenter frames OpenAI's pricing negatively.

evidence.excerpt: This whole process worked about 10 times faster than doing it without GPT. At the end of a couple of hours I had around 20 pages of correct proofs and all the code needed to do numerical simulations related to the proofs.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly reports a major productivity gain and good outputs from GPT in mathematical research work.

evidence.excerpt: Sure, Claude Code and Codex can write (most of) the code for me - but the amount of technical knowledge I need to decide what and how to build remains enormous.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says Claude Code can write most of the code, which is a clear positive capability assessment despite the human-skill caveat.
The commenter explicitly says Codex can write most of the code, which is a clear positive capability assessment despite the human-skill caveat.

evidence.excerpt: Before AI I would have to spend several hours or days learning RouterOS, for example, to be able to configure a datacenter-class Mikrotik router. With Claude that became a 20 minute job and I learned a lot about routing configuration in the process

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says Claude reduced a multi-hour or multi-day task to 20 minutes and was educational in the process.
